content.text: What it is
Agent trace retention and PII redaction policy

defines two things together: how long agent traces are stored (retention), and which span attributes must be removed or masked before traces enter storage (redaction). The two concerns are inseparable because the retention duration determines the scope of PII exposure risk — keeping redacted traces for seven years is safe, but keeping raw traces with verbatim user queries for the same period creates unacceptable liability. A combined policy specifies retention tiers, the redaction transform applied before each tier, which attributes are always deleted, which are pseudonymised, and which are kept intact.
Why it matters

Agentic AI traces are uniquely sensitive because they are a verbatim record of the conversation between a user and the AI system, including raw prompts, intermediate reasoning steps, tool call inputs and outputs, and model responses. Unlike a typical HTTP trace that records a URL and status code, an agent trace may contain a patient's medical history, a user's private financial details, or the contents of a confidential document the agent was asked to summarise. Without explicit redaction, this data flows into the observability backend — often with weaker access controls than production databases — and persists for months or years, dramatically expanding the blast radius of a data breach.
Benefits
Limits PII exposure in the observability backend: engineers get timing, status, and token cost data without seeing raw user content.
Enables long compliance retention periods by ensuring the archived traces no longer constitute personal data under GDPR.
Satisfies HIPAA audit control requirements by keeping PHI only in the restricted clinical log, not the general trace backend.
Reduces breach impact: a compromise of the trace backend does not expose user content, only metadata.
Enables GDPR right-to-erasure fulfilment by removing the pseudonym from the redaction map, effectively anonymising all retained traces.
Trade-offs and limitations
Redaction removes the content that is most useful for debugging prompt-related failures — engineers cannot see what the user actually asked.
Static attribute allowlists become stale as the application evolves: new attributes with PII may slip through until the policy is updated.
Pseudonymisation requires maintaining a secure pseudonym-to-identity mapping, which itself must be tightly access-controlled.
Redaction in the OTel Collector pipeline adds CPU overhead proportional to the transform complexity and span throughput.
Incomplete redaction policies — missing a single attribute that contains PII — can create a compliance violation for the entire retention period.
How it works
Agent spans arrive at the OTel Collector with raw attributes including user-facing content. An
allowlist transform processor
runs before all exporter pipelines and applies three operations to every span: (1)
delete
for attributes that must not exist in any storage backend (raw user query text, model response content); (2)
mask
for attributes that should be pseudonymised (user identifier → HMAC hash); (3)
keep
for safe operational attributes (span timing, status, token count, tool name, error type). Attributes not on the allowlist are deleted by default.

The raw content that is deleted from traces is optionally written to a separate, restricted clinical or content log with tighter access controls, shorter retention (30 days), and a separate audit trail. This gives authorised investigators access to raw content for specific incidents without exposing it to the general observability backend.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Agent trace PII redaction pipeline: collector strips PII before hot storage and compliance archive, raw PII goes to separate restricted log

content.text: The OTel Collector's transform processor redacts PII before traces reach any storage backend. Redacted traces flow to both hot storage (Tempo, 30–90 days) and the compliance archive (S3 WORM, 1–7 years). Raw PII content, if retained at all, goes only to the restricted PHI log with separate access controls and short retention.
When to use it
Any agent system where user input may contain personal data — this is virtually all consumer-facing AI systems.
When operating under GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA, or any data minimisation requirement.
When trace retention exceeds 90 days — the longer the retention window, the higher the breach exposure without redaction.
When engineers who debug the system should not have access to raw user data (separation of duties requirement).
How to implement
Audit all span attributes emitted by your agent code. Classify each as: safe (keep), PII (delete or pseudonymise), or uncertain (treat as PII until confirmed safe).
Define an explicit allowlist of safe attributes. Configure the OTel Collector transform processor to delete all attributes not on the allowlist.
For user identifiers, apply HMAC-SHA256 with a per-deployment secret key to produce a deterministic pseudonym. Store the secret in a KMS-managed secret.
Configure the compliance-tier exporter to write pseudonymised traces to the WORM S3 bucket, and the hot-storage exporter to write to Tempo.
If raw content must be retained for incident investigation, route it to a separate restricted log with access limited to a security response team and retention of 30 days.
Run the span-schema linter in CI with a PII pattern check to catch new attributes that contain personal data before they reach the collector.
Review the redaction allowlist quarterly as part of the platform governance process, and after any major feature launch that changes span attribute patterns.
Examples

# OTel Collector: redaction via transform processor (OTTL)
processors:
  transform/redact_pii:
    error_mode: ignore
    trace_statements:
      - context: span
        statements:
          # Delete raw content attributes
          - delete_key(attributes, "user.query")
          - delete_key(attributes, "agent.response")
          - delete_key(attributes, "tool.input")
          - delete_key(attributes, "tool.output")
          - delete_key(attributes, "llm.prompt")
          - delete_key(attributes, "llm.completion")
          # Pseudonymise user identifier
          - set(attributes["user.pseudonym"],
              SHA256(Concat([attributes["user.id"], ""])))
          - delete_key(attributes, "user.id")
          - delete_key(attributes, "user.email")
          # Keep safe operational attributes (implicitly: all others deleted below)

  # Allowlist: drop any attribute NOT in the safe list
  filter/allowlist:
    error_mode: ignore
    traces:
      span:
        - 'attributes["llm.token_count_total"] == nil and
           attributes["span.kind"] == nil'
        # Better: use transform to delete-all-except pattern

pipelines:
  traces:
    processors: [transform/redact_pii]
    exporters: [tempo, s3/compliance]

# Attribute audit: identify PII candidates in span schema
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import ReadableSpan
import re

# Patterns that suggest PII in attribute names or values
PII_ATTR_PATTERNS = [
    r"user\.(id|email|name|phone|address)",
    r"patient\..*",
    r"customer\.(id|name|email)",
    r"query$",      # raw user input
    r"prompt$",     # raw LLM prompt
    r"response$",   # raw LLM response
    r"input$",      # tool input (may contain user data)
    r"output$",     # tool output (may contain PII from external APIs)
]

def audit_span_attrs(span: ReadableSpan) -> list[str]:
    """Return list of attribute keys that may contain PII."""
    suspicious = []
    for key in span.attributes or {}:
        for pattern in PII_ATTR_PATTERNS:
            if re.search(pattern, key, re.IGNORECASE):
                suspicious.append(key)
                break
    return suspicious
Real-world example
A legal document assistant processes contracts uploaded by law firm clients. Early in deployment, the platform team discovers that the agent is emitting
tool.output

attributes containing extracted contract clauses — verbatim legal content that constitutes attorney-client privileged material. These attributes flow into the shared Tempo trace backend, accessible to all engineers who can query traces.
The team deploys the transform processor with
tool.output

on the delete list. Historical traces with the raw content are purged from Tempo (within the 30-day retention window) and the WORM compliance archive (redacted versions replace them using the Glacier restore + overwrite pattern). The span-schema linter gains a new rule that flags any attribute whose name ends in
output
or
content
as requiring explicit review before production.
Common mistakes
Using an attribute denylist (specific names to delete) instead of an allowlist (only these names to keep) — denylists miss new attributes as the code evolves.
Redacting at the application layer before emitting spans — this prevents PII from reaching the collector but also prevents the restricted clinical log from capturing it for authorised investigation.
Pseudonymising with a static, publicly known salt — an attacker who obtains the trace data can reverse the pseudonymisation.
Not testing the redaction pipeline with real span samples — many transforms behave unexpectedly on empty or null attribute values.
Keeping the same pseudonym HMAC key forever — if the key is compromised, all historical pseudonyms can be reversed.
Best practices
Use an allowlist approach: define which attributes are safe and delete everything else, rather than trying to enumerate PII attributes.
Keep the HMAC pseudonymisation key in KMS with automatic annual rotation; store the rotation date so pseudonym lookups use the correct key.
Test the redaction pipeline with a golden set of span attribute combinations in CI, not just in production.
Run a quarterly attribute audit: pull a sample of production spans and verify that no PII attributes appear after the transform.
Separate the restricted clinical log clearly from the general trace backend — different storage bucket, different IAM policy, different Grafana data source.
Performance and scalability

The transform processor adds approximately 5–15 % CPU overhead to the collector for complex OTTL statement lists. For high-volume deployments (>100k spans/s per collector), benchmark the transform overhead before deploying to production and scale the collector fleet accordingly. Batch the OTTL statements to minimise evaluation passes per span: a single context block with all statements is cheaper than multiple transform processors chained.

Built-in; no external dependency

HMAC key management
Auto rotation, audit log
S3 Object Lock
WORM compliance archive
Immutable after write
span-schema-lint (custom)
CI PII attribute detection
Blocks PRs that add PII attributes

Allowlist + pseudonymise (recommended)

Attribute redaction allowlist example

Maintaining an allowlist in a separate machine-readable file makes it easy to review in pull requests and run validation against span samples. Below is a representative allowlist for a conversational agent system. Any attribute not in this list should be deleted by the transform processor.

# safe-span-attributes.yaml — allowlist for agent traces
# All other attributes will be deleted by the transform processor.
resource_attributes:
  - service.name
  - service.version
  - service.team
  - deployment.environment
  - cloud.provider
  - cloud.region

span_attributes:
  # Identity (pseudonymised)
  - user.pseudonym      # HMAC of user.id — NOT the real ID
  - compliance.scope    # e.g. hipaa, gdpr, none

  # Agent orchestration (safe metadata)
  - agent.use_case
  - agent.iteration
  - agent.long_running

  # LLM metrics (counts/rates, not content)
  - llm.model
  - llm.token_count_input
  - llm.token_count_output
  - llm.token_count_total
  - llm.latency_ms

  # Tool invocation (safe metadata only)
  - tool.name
  - tool.version
  - tool.duration_ms
  - agent.tool_error    # boolean, not error message

  # Standard OTel
  - http.status_code
  - http.method
  - db.system
  - span.kind
  - error.type

# Explicitly banned (belt-and-suspenders)
banned_attributes:
  - user.id
  - user.email
  - user.name
  - user.query
  - agent.response
  - llm.prompt
  - llm.completion
  - tool.input
  - tool.output
Security and reliability

The redaction pipeline is a security control: if it fails open (e.g., collector crash bypasses the transform), PII reaches storage. Configure the collector to fail closed by routing through the transform processor with
error_mode: propagate
for the primary pipeline, and only allowing the restricted PHI log pipeline to operate without transform. Monitor the
otelcol_processor_transform_error_count
metric and alert on any non-zero value — a transform error means the redaction may have been skipped for affected spans.
Key takeaways
Retention and redaction policy must be designed together: the appropriate retention duration depends on whether traces contain PII.
Use an allowlist approach — delete all attributes not explicitly approved — rather than trying to enumerate PII attributes.
The OTel Collector transform processor (OTTL) provides the redaction engine; it must run before every storage exporter pipeline.
Pseudonymise user identifiers with a KMS-managed HMAC key; this enables GDPR erasure by deleting the pseudonym mapping, not the traces.
The redaction pipeline is a security control — monitor for transform errors and alert on any failure.
Test the redaction with real span samples in CI; static analysis of the OTTL config is insufficient — test the actual output.
